title: Make kill_peer resolve peer identifiers safely
description: kill_peer currently accepts raw tmux session:window refs, which can kill the wrong peer when window names differ from peer names. Change the kill path so callers use peer_id/display_name scoped by circle, fail loudly on ambiguity, and preserve legacy tmux refs only as deprecated compatibility.
close_reason: Implemented safe peer-identifier kill path and MCP contract with ambiguity tests

title: Stop hook misses user-interrupt path: peer stuck busy after Esc/cancel
description:
**Symptom**: when a user interrupts mid-turn (Esc / cancel / ctrl-c-equivalent), the Stop hook does not fire and the peer remains `busy` in `list_peers` indefinitely. Subsequent `/notify` to that peer behaves as if it's working when it's actually idle.

**Backends affected**: confirmed in claude-code and codex. opencode + gemini status unverified, likely similar shape since most agents distinguish 'completed' from 'cancelled' at the hook layer.

**Why it matters**:
- Orchestrator routing decisions read `busy` and skip / queue (depending on ask-ack semantics post-PR-99). Stuck-busy peer drops messages or buffers indefinitely.
- Visible UX bug in dashboards / list_peers output.
- ask-ack reminder mechanism reads peer status; a stuck-busy peer never gets reminded because the daemon thinks they're still in a turn.

**Hypothesis on root cause**:
Most agent runtimes have separate hook events for 'turn-completed' vs 'turn-cancelled' (or no hook at all on cancel). Repowire installers wire SessionEnd / Stop hooks to the completion path. Cancel takes a different path, no hook fires, peer registry never sees the busy→online transition.

**Fix shapes (research before committing to one)**:
1. Wire each runtime's cancel hook explicitly. Risk: hook event names may differ, and some runtimes may not emit one at all.
2. Daemon-side timeout: if a peer has been `busy` for >N seconds with no progress signal, auto-flip to online. Catches all cancel paths but requires picking a sensible N and a way to detect 'progress signal' (last hook fire timestamp).
3. ws-hook periodic heartbeat: peer reports its actual status from agent state, not from hook events. Larger change.

**Investigation tasks**:
- Identify each runtime's cancel hook (claude-code, codex, opencode, gemini)
- Reproduce in claude-code: spawn peer, send a long-running prompt, hit Esc, observe `list_peers` for ~60s
- Same in codex
- Pick fix shape based on what hooks exist + how robust each backend is

**Out of scope**:
- ask-ack PR #99 surface (don't touch peer_registry until merged)
- Externally-attached peer story (this bug is about runtime hook paths, not registration)

**Memory references**:
- `feedback_repowire_ws_hook_drop.md` — adjacent issue (peer drops registration), different mechanism
- post-v0.11.0 spawn lifecycle middleware (`installers/post_spawn.py`) — cancel hooks would belong adjacent to this in each installer

title: Refactor MCP send primitives: ask/ack/notify
description:
Restructure peer-to-peer message verbs around a single wire-level send primitive with three intent-flavored tool wrappers.

## Motivation

Current state:
- ask_peer blocks the caller until reply arrives — takes the turn hostage
- notify_peer is what callers actually want most of the time but the verb under-sells it
- No lifecycle for whether messages were picked up or acted on

## Proposed shape

Three tool-level verbs over one wire primitive:
- ask(to, msg, reply_to=None) — non-blocking, enters ack lifecycle
- ack(corr_id, msg=None) — closes a thread, bare or with content
- notify(to, msg) — FYI, no lifecycle, no reminder
- broadcast(msg) — unchanged

Drop ask-as-blocking. Drop await entirely.

## Reminder mechanism

Stop hook reconciliation, lazy-repair style:
- Daemon tracks pickup (next hook fire post-delivery marks ask as picked_up)
- Stop hook checks for unacked picked-up asks
- Inject reminder context block listing unacked asks with corr_ids
- Flip reminded=true after first injection — once-only, never re-nag
- Skip if this turn already ack'd or replied with reply_to
- One turn grace period (pickup → turn → next Stop reminds)
- Cap bundle to 3 most recent + summary line
- Silent when empty
- Respect BUSY state — Notification (idle_prompt) hook may be better injection point

## Out of scope (defer until pain shows up)

- cancel_ask
- pending_asks() introspection
- Bundled reminder ordering policy beyond recency cap

## Migration

- Keep notify_peer as deprecated alias for one release
- ask_peer becomes non-blocking; existing callers that relied on blocking behavior need explicit ack-await pattern (rare)
- Update peer context injection docs to lead with new semantics

## Design context

See conversation thread parking this design. Key insight: every peer-to-peer message is structurally a notification; ask/ack/notify are sender-intent metadata, not distinct protocol primitives. Lifecycle (correlation_id + ack tracking) is what makes ask different from notify, not the wire shape.

title: ws-hook PaneUnsafeError false-positive on daemon ping kills hook permanently
description:
**Symptom**: Peers vanish from `list_peers` after hours of interaction, agent still alive in tmux. Memory `feedback_repowire_ws_hook_drop.md` documents 'restart the pane' workaround.

**Evidence (2026-05-09 investigation)**:
- `~/.cache/repowire/logs/ws-hook-*.log`: 22 occurrences of `Pane %X dead on ping, exiting` across 22 different panes
- `~/.repowire/daemon.log`: 9 `update_peer_status: peer not found` entries (stop/notification hooks trying to update status for peers the daemon dropped after the ws-hook gave up)
- 406 `keepalive ping timeout` stacks in daemon.log are all from `relay_client.py` (daemon→repowire.io outbound), unrelated to local peer vanishing — false lead

**Root cause**: `_is_pane_safe` (`repowire/hooks/websocket_hook.py:116-129`) uses the wrong primitive.

It compares live `tmux display-message -p '#{pane_current_command}'` against `_expected_command` snapshotted ONCE at startup. `pane_current_command` is the **foreground process** in the pane, not the parent agent. When the agent shells out for tool calls (git, python subprocess, npm, task() subagents, etc.) the foreground briefly becomes that subprocess. If the daemon's app-level ping handler runs during a shell-out window, `_is_pane_safe` returns False, the ping handler at line 240-241 raises `PaneUnsafeError`, which propagates to `main()` line 322 → `return 0` → **permanent hook exit**. Harness in tmux keeps running, daemon drops the peer.

**Why prior fixes didn't help**:
- #85 (merged 2026-05-04) correctly fixed the reconnect-exhaustion exit path (50-attempt cap removed, indefinite retry). That's a different exit path. Verified current source AND installed 0.10.2 both have the indefinite-retry fix.
- The `PaneUnsafeError` exit path was untouched by #85 and is the active production failure mode.

**Why the bug worsens 'after hours'**: hours-long sessions accumulate many ping × shell-out coincidences. Daemon ping_interval × agent-tool-use rate determines hit probability per ping. P(no kill in N pings) decays exponentially.

**Fix design (paired between repowire-claude-code + repowire-codex)**:

1. **Ping-handler should not be a one-sample kill switch** (surgical safety):
   - At `websocket_hook.py:240-241`, do NOT `raise PaneUnsafeError` on a single ping false-negative.
   - Send pong with `pane_alive=false` to inform the daemon, log at WARNING.
   - Require N consecutive ping-time pane-unsafe results before exiting (e.g. N=3).
   - Query/notify/broadcast safety can stay stricter — those guards prevent spurious INJECTION, not hook lifetime; they don't kill the hook.

2. **Replace `_is_pane_safe` foreground-command check with process-tree liveness**:
   - Walk the pane's process subtree starting from `tmux display-message -p '#{pane_pid}'` (the pane shell PID).
   - Build a parent→children map from `ps -axo pid=,ppid=,comm=` (portable across macOS/Linux; recursive pgrep flags differ between platforms).
   - Pane is safe if the subtree contains a process whose comm matches `_expected_command` (or backend-specific allow-list).
   - Detects real pane reuse (original agent gone, only shell or new command remains) without false-positiving on transient shell-outs.

3. **Cached agent PID as an anchor + subtree fallback**:
   - At startup, after snapshotting expected command, also try to discover and cache the agent's PID by scanning the pane's subtree for the matching comm.
   - Fast path on safety check: `os.kill(cached_pid, 0)` — process alive?
   - On stale cache (kill returned ESRCH or perm error), fall back to subtree rescan.
   - Do NOT rely on `pane_pid` directly — that's the shell.

4. **Consecutive-failure fallback**: if process-tree inspection errors or tmux returns no `pane_pid`, treat as unsafe only after N consecutive failures rather than first-error. Keep `PaneUnsafeError` permanent for confirmed-takeover (agent gone from subtree, only shell or new command), but make confirmation stronger than `pane_current_command != expected`.

**Test plan**:
- Mock `ps` output + tmux `pane_pid` output. Cases:
  - Foreground is `git`/`python` but pane subtree contains `claude` → safe.
  - Foreground is `claude`, subtree contains `claude` → safe.
  - Foreground is `zsh`, subtree no longer contains `claude` → unsafe.
  - User killed claude, started gemini in same pane → subtree contains `gemini` not `claude` → unsafe (correct takeover detection).
  - tmux returns no pane_pid (e.g. pane killed) → unsafe after consecutive-failure threshold, not first error.
- Ping handler: assert single ping-time pane-unsafe does NOT raise; assert raise after N consecutive.
- Existing tests: `test_hooks.py` shell-denylist case still passes (no `_expected_command` set).

**Out of scope** (file separately):
- Daemon-side health visibility for degraded peers (e.g. dashboard surfaces stale-mapping peers). Codex's recommendation: not in this PR, separate issue.
- OpenCode 50-cap removal (`installers/opencode.py:207`) — separate issue, related but distinct (TS plugin, different transport).
- Async-native tmux subprocess calls (`asyncio.create_subprocess_exec` instead of `subprocess.run` + `to_thread`) — file-quality improvement, unrelated to this bug, can be its own PR.
- Registry merge-on-pane-match — codex's earlier idea, also separate.

**Refs**: GitHub #84 (original report, partially addressed by #85), GitHub #85 (reconnect-exhaustion fix, merged 2026-05-04). This issue tracks the second exit path that #85 didn't cover.

**False leads documented for future investigators**:
- Relay-client `websockets.connect()` keepalive (relay_client.py:125) IS noisy and worth fixing for log signal-to-noise, but does not cause local peer vanishing.
- Aggressive registry eviction at `peer_registry.py:447-465` (`register_peer` same-name purge) is the correct response when the ws goes truly dead, not a bug. It only becomes user-visible because the hook dies prematurely.
- Threadpool contention in `_tmux_send_keys` (subprocess.run + time.sleep wrapped in to_thread) — codex pushed back on this being a proximate cause; each hook process has its own loop+threadpool. Worth fixing for cleanliness but not for this bug.
notes:
**CORRECTION (codex review, 2026-05-09)**:

The 'keepalive ping timeout' evidence in ~/.repowire/daemon.log does NOT support the original hook→daemon hypothesis. Verified against installed 0.10.2 and current source:

1. **Daemon DOES set explicit None/None already**: `repowire/cli.py:56` and `:1327` already pass `ws_ping_interval=None, ws_ping_timeout=None` to uvicorn.run. Both source tree AND installed 0.10.2 in `~/.local/share/uv/tools/repowire/` match. There's a deprecated `daemon start -f` path + `repowire.daemon.app` module path that would still use uvicorn defaults, but the active launchd service is `repowire serve` which is fixed.

2. **The 406 timeouts are from `relay_client.py`, not `/ws` agent endpoint**: log entries show 'Relay connection lost, reconnecting in 2s' adjacent to the ConnectionClosedError stacks. `repowire/daemon/relay_client.py:125-130` calls `websockets.connect()` without explicit ping settings → gets websockets library defaults (20s/20s) → fails under any network jitter on the daemon→repowire.io relay link. This is **noisy but unrelated to local peer disappearance**.

**Revised hypothesis for the original symptom (peers vanishing locally after hours)**:
- The relay_client noise is real but is its own bug (file separately or address as part 2 of this issue).
- The local-peer-vanishing problem more likely comes from **registry identity/status retention**: aggressive same-name eviction at `peer_registry.py:447-465` + name-reclaim at line 302 + 24h stale eviction. These trigger on any OFFLINE→reconnect→new-peer_id cycle, which can happen for reasons besides ping timeouts (env var loss across hook restarts, lifecycle events, race in allocate_and_register).

**Revised fix bundle**:

1. **Fix relay_client keepalive** (`repowire/daemon/relay_client.py:125`): pass explicit `ping_interval=20, ping_timeout=60` to `websockets.connect()`. Eliminates the 406 noisy timeout stacks. Doesn't fix peer-vanishing but stops the false-flag in logs.

2. **Async-native tmux subprocess calls** in `websocket_hook.py` (`_tmux_send_keys`, `_get_pane_command`, `_is_pane_safe`): use `asyncio.create_subprocess_exec` + `await asyncio.sleep` instead of `subprocess.run` + `time.sleep` wrapped in `asyncio.to_thread`. Codex's read: this is still worth doing for latency/cleanliness, but is NOT the proximate cause of pong failures (each hook process has its own loop+threadpool). It does affect app-level ping/notify responsiveness in `handle_message()` though.

3. **Registry merge-on-pane-match** in `allocate_and_register` (NOT legacy `register_peer`): when a new peer registers with same `(pane_id, circle, backend)` as an existing OFFLINE peer (and path/display_name agree), reuse the existing `peer_id` instead of pruning + creating new. Codex's caveat: pane_ids CAN be reused after pane death or tmux server restart, so do not blindly merge — only merge if existing peer is OFFLINE (not ONLINE/BUSY) and path/backend/circle agree. This is the actual fix for peers-vanishing-after-hours.

**Sequence (codex's recommendation)**:
- PR1: relay_client keepalive + async-native tmux injection + keepalive policy cleanup. Tests around hook message handling and daemon startup args.
- PR2: registry identity semantics (merge-on-pane-match). Separate because it changes user-visible peer identity and can introduce subtle stale-pane bugs — needs its own review.

**Evidence still to pull before coding**:
- Confirm there's no second daemon launch path on this machine (deprecated `daemon start -f`, `repowire.daemon.app` direct invocation) — only the launchd `serve` should be live.
- Log correlation: grep around each timeout for relay-related vs hook-related context (we've confirmed relay so far; want to rule out the hook path entirely).
- For peer-vanishing specifically: timestamps of `Peer registered` / `Pruned offline peer` / `evicted N stale offline peers` in daemon.log, correlated with when Prass observed peers disappearing.
close_reason: Fixed by GH PR #95 (merged 2026-05-09 at 70d7445). Subtree-based pane safety + ping consecutive-failure threshold + cached PID with periodic rescan + timeouts on all subprocess calls. Paired diagnosis with repowire-codex; 5 gemini-bot review threads addressed in second commit.

comments.author: Prass, The Nomadic Coder
comments.text: Investigation update from Codex: sampled/grepped ~/.repowire/daemon.log and the 406 'keepalive ping timeout' traces are from repowire/daemon/relay_client.py while reading the outbound relay websocket, not the local FastAPI /ws agent endpoint. Active launchd service is /Users/prass/.local/bin/repowire serve (0.10.2), and both installed/current serve paths pass uvicorn.run(..., ws_ping_interval=None, ws_ping_timeout=None). There are still deprecated launch paths with uvicorn defaults: repowire cli daemon start -f and python -m repowire.daemon.app. The peer-vanish symptom is still real, but this log evidence points to relay churn/noisy reconnects rather than hook-to-daemon keepalive as proximate cause. Continue investigating local registry/status lifecycle separately: default list_peers hides OFFLINE peers, lazy_repair can mark websocket-less peers offline, and offline peers are later evicted by prune_max_age_hours or immediately pruned on name reclaim in _build_display_name.

title: Land tmux notification injection escape fix on top of main
description: The existing feat/notify-stuff branch contains a useful tmux injection fix in commit 394fa18 that replaces a bare Escape keypress with an explicit bracketed-paste close sequence during tmux notification injection. That branch is now behind main and includes older ws-hook reconnect behavior, so it cannot be merged directly. Extract the notification injection fix onto a fresh branch from current main, validate it against the current ws-hook reconnect code, and open a clean PR.

title: Fix ws-hook silent deregistration after reconnect exhaustion
description: A Claude peer can remain alive in tmux while its background websocket_hook process exits after exhausting reconnect attempts. When that happens the daemon drops the peer from the active registry, inbound notifications fail or route to fallbacks, and outbound MCP calls can misleadingly appear to work because lazy HTTP registration recreates peer state without restoring the inbound WebSocket transport. Fix this by making ws-hook self-heal instead of exiting permanently after reconnect exhaustion, and surface degraded inbound connectivity clearly enough that operators can diagnose it. Validate against the observed symptom of ws-hook logs ending with 'Exhausted 50 reconnect attempts, exiting'.

title: Telegram bot: persistent peer-switch keyboard with fresh data + retry-on-failure
description: Replace the sticky-/select + inline-button routing model with a persistent Telegram ReplyKeyboardMarkup that's always visible below the compose bar. One tap to switch peers, current target marked with ✦, recents filtered against live daemon /peers data, live placeholder showing who you're talking to. On send failure, refresh the keyboard with fresh peer data and stash a pending-retry intent: the next peer-tap replays the failed message to that peer (cancellable by typing new text). Inline buttons used only for the '…' full-peer expansion. Rationale: real-usage observations (notif-978a6725) — @-tag-in-prose and one-word replies to recent notifiers dominate; sticky /select goes stale; inline buttons add visual noise and scrollback hell. See design convo in session repowire-claude-code 2026-04-23.
design: Persistent ReplyKeyboardMarkup rebuilt on every outgoing bot message. Markers: ✦ current, 💬 recent, … open full picker. Placeholder shows 'msg @target…' or 'No active peer'. Recents = in-memory deque(maxlen=10), filtered against fresh /peers on each rebuild. Failure path: refetch /peers, rebuild keyboard, store pending_retry=(text, expires_at, ~60s TTL); on next peer-switch tap within window, resend original text to new peer; on typed message, clear retry. /keyboard off escape hatch.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Persistent keyboard appears on every bot message; 2. Tap ✦/💬 peer switches target with one tap, no typing; 3. Placeholder reflects current target live; 4. On /notify failure, keyboard refreshes + pending-retry set; 5. Peer-tap during retry window resends original message; 6. Typing new text cancels retry; 7. /keyboard off removes it; 8. Unit tests for keyboard builder, prefix parser, recents dedup+online-filter, retry TTL.
notes: Implemented on feat/telegram-persistent-keyboard. 25 new unit tests, 316 total pass, ruff+ty clean. Not pushed — awaiting Prass review.

title: Adopt beads for issue tracking
description: Replace TodoWrite/markdown TODO lists with beads (bd) for all task tracking in this repo. Includes: initialize .beads/ workspace, commit config.yaml + hooks + metadata, update CLAUDE.md with beads workflow section, add session-close protocol requiring git push before calling work complete.
close_reason: Done in working tree: .beads/ initialized with config+hooks+metadata, CLAUDE.md updated with beads section and session-close protocol. Pending commit.

title: Prune stale sessions.json entries on daemon startup
description:
After daemon restart, offline peer records from earlier sessions re-emerge in /peers responses as 'online' until lazy_repair re-evaluates ws status. Observed during v0.11.0 smoke: 30 stale probe peers from sessions.json appeared post-restart with no ws connection, required manual eviction via /kill-peer.

Two fix directions:
1. On daemon startup, drop sessions.json entries older than N hours (24h?) before loading.
2. On daemon startup, mark all loaded peers as offline (status=offline) and let lazy_repair re-mark online only when a ws actually connects.

(2) is cleaner because it removes the lying 'online' window entirely. Status reflects transport reality. Stale records age out naturally via the existing prune_max_age_hours window.

Why: notify_peer/ask_peer to a status='online' peer with no ws returns 500 'No connection for session X' -- same failure mode as the v0.10.6 bug we just fixed, despite the post-spawn-warmup fix being correct.

How to apply: when implementing next daemon refactor, prefer option 2. Touch daemon/peer_registry.py load_from_disk().

title: daemon: /notify should distinguish delivered vs queued vs unknown peer
description: Today /notify returns 200 for both 'delivered to online peer' and 'queued for offline peer', and 404 only when the peer name doesn't resolve at all. Clients (Telegram bot, dashboard) can't tell the difference between a real delivery and a queued-for-later, so they can't surface accurate status (e.g. ⏳ vs 👍). Proposed: richer response shape like {delivered: bool, queued: bool, status: 'online'|'offline'|'unknown'}. Surfaced while designing the Telegram persistent keyboard — bot has to guess offline state from its own cached /peers list instead of getting an authoritative signal from the notify response.
acceptance_criteria: 1. /notify response distinguishes delivered, queued, and unknown-peer cases; 2. Existing 200/404 contract preserved or versioned; 3. Telegram bot and dashboard can consume the new shape without guessing.

title: Silence curl output in tmux rename hooks
description: The after-rename-session/window tmux hooks shell out to curl, which prints the JSON response body ({"ok":true}) to stdout. tmux's run-shell captures that and flashes it in the status bar on every rename — noisy. Fix: redirect curl stdout to /dev/null in repowire/hooks/tmux_rename_hook.sh and the inline curl commands in repowire/hooks/tmux_lifecycle.py.
